reasoning: Output B edges out Output A across several dimensions. Both are high-quality contracts, but B has meaningful advantages: (1) B's postconditions section includes full expect() skeletons with complete test bodies (not just one-liner assertions), making tautology verification concrete and immediately usable. A provides partial skeletons for a subset of PCs. (2) B explicitly contracts a null/undefined tenantId guard (PC-S6), which is a critical security postcondition A omits entirely. (3) B's error cases include ERR-6 (threshold:0 is valid, not an error) and ERR-10 (XSS payload stored as literal), demonstrating sharper edge-case thinking. (4) B's traceability matrix includes full absolute file paths (e.g. apps/api/src/routes/__tests__/alertConfigsRoute.test.js) rather than bare filenames, making it directly actionable. (5) B's error strategy section includes actual pseudocode for the route error dispatch pattern, removing ambiguity for the implementer. A's primary advantage is file:line refs in the consumer map (e.g. :L~38), which B lacks. However, B's superior PC coverage (17 vs 16), tenantId null guard, fuller skeletons, and richer error case set outweigh A's consumer map line precision.

strengths: Consumer map includes file:line references (e.g. useAlertConfigs.js:L~38, AlertConfigForm.jsx:L~52) satisfying expectation 4
Blast radius section names 3 same-file siblings (listAlertConfigs, updateAlertConfig, deleteAlertConfig) and 2 cross-file siblings with precise function signatures
Edge case table (9 entries) is thorough, including Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER overflow analysis and concurrent duplicate POST race condition
Error strategy section explicitly justifies why no transaction is needed
INV-6 specifies that err.message must never be forwarded raw to res.json() — precise and actionable
All 16 PCs traceable in matrix with PENDING status
weaknesses: Postcondition skeletons are partial — only ~7 of 16 PCs get expect() skeletons, leaving 9 PCs without concrete failure assertions
Missing PC for null/undefined tenantId guard — a critical security postcondition that protects against NULL tenant_id insertion
PC-A7 for 409 duplicate category is present but A did not include threshold:0 as explicitly valid (ERR-6 in B) — contracts threshold:0 as an error (ERR-4) which may be wrong
Consumer map line numbers are estimated (L~38, L~52) not verified — marked as such, but reduces confidence

strengths: Full expect() skeletons for all runtime PCs — each skeleton is a complete test body, not just assertions, immediately copy-pasteable
PC-S6 contracts the tenantId null/undefined guard, preventing NULL tenant_id insertion — a security postcondition A omits
ERR-6 explicitly designates threshold:0 as valid (201, not an error) — more accurate than A which treats zero as an error
ERR-10 contracts XSS payload handling explicitly with rationale (store as literal, frontend escapes)
Traceability matrix uses full absolute file paths, making it directly actionable without mental translation
Error strategy section includes concrete route handler pseudocode, removing implementation ambiguity
17 PCs vs A's 16, with tighter cross-tenant isolation test (PC-X2 checks DB directly, not via HTTP list)
Blast radius cross-file siblings include line estimates and two confirmation checks per sibling
weaknesses: Consumer map lacks file:line references — only file paths are given, no line numbers, which fails expectation 4 partially
Consumer map has a self-referential entry (alertConfigsRoute.js as a consumer of itself) which is semantically odd
PC-S7 is structural/not runtime — it maps to 'code review' rather than a runnable test, which weakens the traceability claim slightly
Some blast radius sibling line estimates are clearly approximate (productsRoute.js:~L12) and not verified against actual code
